coredata.dc:title: Porous polyimide/carbon quantum dots/ZnS quantum dots material aerogel for efficient visible-light photocatalytic degradation over oxytetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Qian
Fan, Zijie
Yi, Xibin
Chen, Shiwei
Li, Bing
Luo, Weiyue
coredata.dc:description: The polyimide is a promising semiconductor photocatalyst. However, its photocatalytic activity is poor because of its small specific surface area, low visible light absorption and easy recombination of photoexcited electron and hole. Herein, porous polyimide/carbon quantum dots/ZnS quantum dots material aerogel with excellent photocatalytic performance is prepared via supercritical fluid drying technology. The material aerogel has large surface area and the structure is porous network. The carbon quantum dots increase the visible light absorption of the polyimide aerogel. The ZnS quantum dots change the VB edge, CB edge and band gap of the polyimide aerogel. Both carbon quantum dots and ZnS quantum dots could improve the charge transfer and facilitate the separation of photoexcited electron and hole. The visible-light photocatalytic performance over oxytetracycline of porous polyimide/carbon quantum dots/ZnS quantum dots material aerogel is increased largely compared to pure polyimide aerogel. The photocatalytic degradation mechanism of the polyimide material aerogel is proposed. ·O2 − and h+ play the key role in the photocatalytic degradation. This work supplies an effective method to prepare visible-light photocatalytic materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyimide
Carbon quantum dots
ZnS quantum dots
Aerogel
Photocatalytic property

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of PI/CQDs/ZnS quantum dots aerogels

Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful for the supports of the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (Grant No. ZR2019BEM025 ); Young Doctor Cooperation Foundation of Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences) (Grant No. 2019BSHZ0016 ); College Students Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (Grant No. S201910431075 ; xj201810431071 ); Science-Education-Industry Integration Innovation Pilot Project of Qilu University of Technology (Grant No. 2020KJC-GH13 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis scheme of PI/CQDs/ZnS aerogel.
The XRD spectra of PI, PI/CQDs, PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels.
The SEM images of PI(a), PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5(b), PI/CQDs/ZnS-1(c) and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels(d); Elemental mapping C(e) and Zn(f) of PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5.
XPS spectra of N 1 s (a), C 1 s (b) and Zn 2p (c) of PI, PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels.
VBXPS(a), Kubelka-Munk plots (b), schematic band structures (c) of PI, PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels.
BET of PI, PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels.
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of PI, PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels.
Photoluminescence spectra of PI, PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels.

Photocatalytic degradation curves of OTC over PI, PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels(a); Cycle runs for the degradation of OTC over PI/CQDs-1 under visible light (λ >420 nm)(b).
Visible-light photocatalytic degradation curves over OTC of PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 aerogel with and without scavengers.
The photocatalytic mechanism of PI/CQDs/ZnS aerogel under visible light.
BET parameters of PI, PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels.

head.ce:title: Porous polyimide/carbon quantum dots/ZnS quantum dots material aerogel for efficient visible-light photocatalytic degradation over oxytetracycline

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Processing and Testing Technology of Glass and Functional Ceramics
School of Material Science and Engineering
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The polyimide is a promising semiconductor photocatalyst. However, its photocatalytic activity is poor because of its small specific surface area, low visible light absorption and easy recombination of photoexcited electron and hole. Herein, porous polyimide/carbon quantum dots/ZnS quantum dots material aerogel with excellent photocatalytic performance is prepared via supercritical fluid drying technology. The material aerogel has large surface area and the structure is porous network. The carbon quantum dots increase the visible light absorption of the polyimide aerogel. The ZnS quantum dots change the VB edge, CB edge and band gap of the polyimide aerogel. Both carbon quantum dots and ZnS quantum dots could improve the charge transfer and facilitate the separation of photoexcited electron and hole. The visible-light photocatalytic performance over oxytetracycline of porous polyimide/carbon quantum dots/ZnS quantum dots material aerogel is increased largely compared to pure polyimide aerogel. The photocatalytic degradation mechanism of the polyimide material aerogel is proposed. ·O2 − and h+ play the key role in the photocatalytic degradation. This work supplies an effective method to prepare visible-light photocatalytic materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Pharmaceutical products cause serious water pollution, which is harmful to human health. Oxytetracycline (OTC) is a typical antibiotic with a huge dosage in the world. Over last decades, many countries have paid much attention to the pharmaceutical pollution of OTC. However, due to stable chemical structure and resistance to biodegradation, traditional water treatment was not efficient to remove it. Consequently, it is urgent to seek an efficiently enviromental technology to decompose OTC. Photocatalysis is a promising technology by using renewable and clean solar energy [1,2].

Polyimide (PI) is a kind of polymer with excellent comprehensive property [3–6]. Owing to stable aromatic heterocyclic structural units, the polyimide has better heat resistance, mechanical and electrical properties than other polymer materials. Polyimide is widely used in all areas in modern society. Its products include flexible film, coating, advanced composite materials, adhesive and so on. In recent years, non-toxic and low-cost organic polymer photocatalysts have become a research hotspot in the field of photocatalysis [7–11]. Polyimide can absorb visible light and their valence band (VB) could be regulated easily, which has aroused extensive interest of scientists [12–17]. Chu prepared PI photocatalyst by simple thermal polymerization and found that PI photocatalyst had good light absorption and photocatalytic activity. The band gap could be regulated according to the temperature change [18]. However, when polyimide is used as photocatalyst, the light absorption efficiency and the charge transport rate of the polymer semiconductor are low, resulting in high recombination rate of photogenerated carriers and low photocatalytic efficiency [19,20]. Recently, scientists used nanoparticles to encounter these problems such as perovskite and carbon quantum dots [21].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a kind of carbon nano materials with a size of <10 nm. They have been studied widely because of their excellent conductivity, high quantum yield, strong chemical stability and special photoelectric properties [22–24]. CQDs have unique up-conversion performance. That is, they could emit high-frequency emission light using low-frequency excitation light, such as converting visible light into ultraviolet, which could be absorbed and utilized by wide-band gap semiconductors to improve light absorption efficiency [25–28]. Pan prepared CQDs/p-type CuAlO2/n-type ZnO composites and found that photocurrent of composites was increased owing to unique up-converted photoluminescence property of the CQDs [29]. Yu prepared CQDs/TiO2 nanosheet (TNS) composites and found that the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TNS composites was largely increased. They confirmed that the unique up-converted property of CQDs favored the enhancement of photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TNS composites [30].

ZnS is an important II - VI semiconductor photocatalyst, which has been widely studied because of its rich nano-sized morphology, stable structure, good physical properties, high conductivity and special photocatalytic properties [31–37]. According to the energy band structure of ZnS semiconductor, it can be concluded that the conduction band (CB) potential of ZnS semiconductor is more negative than that of other semiconductor materials [38–42]. That is, the electrons generated by photoexcitation of ZnS semiconductor materials have strong reduction ability. At the same time, the valence band potential of ZnS material is close to the oxidation potential of oxygen, which weakens the oxidation ability of holes generated by ZnS photoexcitation [43,44]. The activity of ZnS in photodegradation of water to obtain hydrogen under ultraviolet light has long been known [45–47]. The excellent photocatalytic performance of ZnS depends on its unique optical properties: (1) the electron-hole pairs of ZnS could generate quickly under UV excitation;(2) the excited electron has a very low negative reduction potential;(3) ZnS has strong stability under light excitation. However, due to the wide band gap of ZnS, it can only be excited by ultraviolet light [48–50]. Meanwhile, the separation efficiency of electron-hole pair was low, and most photogenerated carriers did not play the role of redox. Moreover, ZnS particles aggregated seriously, which could influence the photocatalytic activity [51–53]. In addition, the photocorrosion caused by the stacked electrons from the CB resulted in low recycling [54–56]. These problems greatly limited the practical application of ZnS as photocatalyst.

In order to solve these problems above, the polyimide/CQDs/ZnS quantum dots material aerogel was prepared. The polyimide aerogels had large surface area, which had more active sites and could be used as good carrier for CQDs and ZnS quantum dots. The porous structure of polyimide aerogel could anchor the quantum dots, thus reduced the aggregation and improved the recycling stability. CQDs had good up-conversion property, which could increase the visible light absorption of the polyimide aerogel. In addition, ZnS possessed wide band gap, which had strong redox ability. So the good photocatalytic property of the material aerogel could be expected.

3,3ˊ,4,4ˊ-Biphenyltetracarboxylic dianhydride (BPDA), di-(4-aminophenyl)ether (ODA), N,N-dimethyllformamide (DMF), trimesoylchloride (TMC)，OTC, ethylene glycol, zinc nitrate hexahydrate，thiourea，trimethylamine (Et3N), and acetic anhydride were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai,China).

Acetic acid (1.2 mL) and chitosan (1 g, 0.1 mmol) were added to 50 mL autoclave, then the autoclave is heated to 160 °C. Twenty four hours later, the suspension was cooled to room temperature. Then the suspension was centrifuged at 12000 RPM for 5 min. Subsequently, the centrifuged suspension containing CQDs was dried under vacuum at 90 °C to obtain CQDs.

Zinc acetate (0.29 g, 1.6 mmol) and thiourea (3.04 g, 40 mmol) were added into deionized water (40 mL). The solution was stirring for 30 min at room temperature, and then transferred to 60 mL hydrothermal autoclave. The temperature of the autoclave was adjusted to 140 °C. 5 h later, the temperature was cooled to room temperature. Followingly, the solution was centrifuged and dried at 80 °C for 10 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PI/CQDs/ZnS quantum dots aerogels
ce:section.ce:para: ODA (1.50 g, 7.5 mmol) was put into 250 mL three-necked round bottom flask with NMP (80 mL), which was stirring for 30 min at room temperature. CQDs (0.039 g) was dispersed in the solution. 5 min later, ZnS quantum dots was put and kept stirring for 10 min. Subsequently, BPDA(2.39 g, 8.0 mmol) was added in with continuous stirring to complete dissolution. Then acetic anhydride (11.07 mL) was put and stirred for 5 min. Followingly, Et3N (2.04 mL) was added. After 15 min, the solution of NMP (10 mL) with TMC (0.065 g, 0.24 mmol) was added and kept stirring for another 5 min. The solution was transferred to a beaker and the gel could be obtained after 1 h. The solvent within the gels was exchanged with ethanol 7 times (for 24 h each time). Then, the wet gel was dried by supercritical CO2 at 40°Cand 8.2 MPa. Finally, PI/CQDs/ZnS quantum dots aerogel was prepared. The synthesis scheme of PI/CQDs/ZnS aerogel was shown in Fig. 1 .
The products containing ZnS quantum dots of 0.5, 1.0, and 3.0 wt% were denoted by PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1, and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3, respectively. Pure PI aerogel was also prepared and denoted by PI.

Typically, aerogel samples (0.1 g) were added into OTC solution (10.0 mg/L, 100.0 mL) with continuous stirring. Then the solution was stirred for 1 h under dark to make adsorption/desorption equilibrium before visible-light illumination. 1.0 mL solution were filtered by 0.22 μm Millipore filters at a pre-determined time interval to remove the photocatalyst.The photocatalytic degradation measurement was made by a 250 W mercury lamp with a UV cutoff filter (λ ≥ 420 nm). The absorbance of OTC at 364 nm wavelength was measured on a UV–vis spectrometer and used for the analysis of degradation efficiency. The degradation rates of the photocatalysts were obtained according to C/C0, where C0 was the starting concentration of OTC solution in adsorption/desorption equilibrium and C was residual concentration after irradiation, respectively.

The photocatalysts were then filtered and washed by ultrapure water and ethanol several times for next use. Three cycles of photocatalytic degradation for OTC were measured to estimate the cycling stability of photocatalysts.

X-ray diffractometer (XRD) spectra was obtained by a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer equipped with a θ/2θ Bragg-Brentano geometry. Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra was performed on a Nicolet NEXUS870 spectrometer. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were obtained with a Hitachi S4800 FE-SEM system. Brunner-Emmet-Teller (BET) specific surface area was measured on Micromeritics ASAP 2020 by N2 adsorption-desorption isothermals. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured using PHI 5000 Versa Probe X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. UV–vis spectra were achieved using a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra was measured on a Horiba Scientific FluoroMax-4 fluorometer spectrometer. Fluorescence emission spectrum was achieved on Horiba Jobin Yvon Data Station by time-correlated single-photon counting.

Fig. 2 showed the XRD spectra of PI, PI/CQDs, PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels. According to the figure, typical wide diffraction peaks at 15.6° and 23.1° occurred on the PI, showing the structure of PI aerogel was noncrystalline. It is noted that the intensity of the peak at 24o of PI/CQDs aerogels increased compared to that of PI. This is because that this peak was also corresponding to the structure of CQDs. The peaks at 24o of polyimide aerogel and CQDs were overlapped. By comparison, three sharp and typical diffraction peaks at 28.9°, 47.6°, and 57.1°, occurred on the curves of PI/CQDs/ZnS aerogels. The peaks at 28.9, 47.6,and 57.1, were corresponding to (111,220,311) crystal faces of ZnS [57,58]. As the increase of ZnS quantum dots, the typical diffraction peak became sharper and the intensity increased. It is noted that two typical wide diffraction peaks of PI changed into a wider diffraction peak in the XRD spectra of PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3. It was reasonable that CQDs and ZnS influenced the crystal structure.

Fig. 3 displayed the SEM images of PI, PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels. Elemental mapping C(e) and Zn(f) of PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5 were also shown. According to the Fig. 3(a)-(d), the images of PI aerogel, PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels had the similar structures, which were porous network. As seen from (e) and (f), C element and Zn element were dispersed in the polymer matrix. The result confirmed that the CQDs and ZnSquantum dots were covered on the network of the PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels. Because of the strong quantum confinement, CQDs and ZnS quantum dots could be anchoring at the surface of the aerogel tightly by π -π stacking interactions [59].As the the content of the quantum dots increased, more quantum dots were found in the PI aerogels. It is well known that the porous structures possessed more active sites and could efficiently absorp little molecules such as organic pollutant and O2, which benefited the photocatalysis [60,61].

Fig. 4 showed the XPS spectra of N 1 s (a) and C 1 s (b) of PI, PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels. According to Fig. 4(a), N peak at 400.1ev occurred on PI curves. This N atom was ascribed to that in the imide of the polyimide main chain. It is noted that the position of N peak shifted up on the curves of PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels, showing that the quantum dots could influence the chemical environment. As seen from Fig. 4(b), C 1 s peak in the PI was changed to two peaks at 284.8ev and 288.2ev. These two peaks were corresponding to adventitious carbon and sp2-hybridized carbon atoms, respectively. The peaks at 288.2ev moved up in the PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels, implying that the chemical state of C atoms was also influenced by the CQDs. The result above indicated that quantum dots had the interaction with the polyimide. According to Fig. 4(c), Zn peak occurred on the curves of PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels.

Fig. 5 showed VBXPS(a), Kubelka-Munk plots (b), schematic band structures (c) of PI, PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels. According to figure (a), the VB edge of pure PI aerogel was 1.76ev. After the addition of the CQDs and ZnS quantum dots, the VB edges of PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 increased to 1.92ev, 2.45ev and 2.37ev, respectively. The VB of PI/CQDs/ZnS aerogel became more positive compared to that of PI aerogel. The high VB edge implied strong oxidizing ability. The figure (b) displayed that the band gap of PI aerogel was 2.71ev. Owing to the introduction of CQDs and ZnS quantum dots, the VB edges of PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels were decreased to 1.92ev, 2.45ev and 2.37ev, respectively. The band gaps of PI/CQDs/ZnS aerogels got narrower than that of PI aerogel. The narrow band gap stands for stronger absorption of visible light. The energy map was displayed on figure (c). According to the article, smaller band gaps and higher VB bands could possess stronger photocatalytic performance [2,62]. It was found that PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 owned the smallest band gap and the highest VB. Thus, we could conclude that the photocatalytic activity of PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 could be enhanced largely. Notably,the band gap and VB of PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 was bigger than that of PI/CQDs/ZnS-1. Meanwhile, the VB of PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 was lower than that of PI/CQDs/ZnS-1.This is because that more quantum dots could gather to large particles, which influenced the absorption of the visible light.

Fig. 6 showed BET of PI, PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels. The hysteresis loops was found on BET curves of PI, PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels, confirming that the PI and PI/CQDs/ZnS aerogels possessed mesoporous structures [63]. According to the report, mesoporous structure could not only benefit mass transfer but also facilitate the separation of photogenerated electron and hole [64]. It is noted that the hysteresis loop of PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 was bigger than that of other aerogels. Meanwhile, the position of hysteresis loop shifted up. According to Table 1 , the specific surface area of PI/CQDs/ZnS aerogels were larger than that of pure PI aerogel. Especially, the surface area of PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 was 434 m2/g, increased by 185 m2/g compared to pure PI aerogel. The large surface area was beneficial for polyimide aerogels to absorb small molecules to their surface. Meanwhile, polyimide aerogels with larger surface area owned more active sites, which could have the reaction the molecules on their surface more easily. Thus the large surface would favor the enhancement of the photocatalytic property. It could be found that the surface area of PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 was smaller than that of PI/CQDs/ZnS-1. This is because that the deposition and accumulation of excessive quantum dots could aggregate to large particles and could insert to the pores of the polyimide aerogels.

Fig. 7 showed UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of PI, PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels. According to the figure, the absorption intensity between 400 and 700 nm of PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels was enhanced compared by that of PI aerogel. Meanwhile, the light absorption edge of PI/CQDs/ZnS aerogel had red shift. Especially, the intensity of PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 aerogels increased evidently and the light absorption edge of PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 aerogels got over 800 nm. This result confirmed the CQDs and ZnS quantum dots increased the light absorption activity. The reason is as follows: firstly, owing to the upconvertion of CQDs, the visible light with long wavelength and low energy was changed to ultroviolet light with short wavelength and high energy, which could enhance the visible light absorption efficiency of the polyimide aerogel; secondly, the heterojunction between polyimide aerogel and ZnS quantum dots facilitated the light absorption; thirdly, porous structure of polyimide aerogel could scatter the photon to the inner cavity, which could be captured and thus improve the light absorption; fourthly, the light could be refracted and scattered repeatedly in the inner cavity, which could also promote the light absorption of the polyimide aerogel.

Fig. 8 showed the photoluminescence spectra of PI, PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels. According to the figure, the fluorescence intensity of PI/CQDs/ZnS aerogel decreased compared to that of PI aerogel. The low intensity means that it is difficult for the photogenerated electron and hole to recombine. This is because that: firstly, CQDs had good electrical conductivity, which could transfer the electron to the surface of the polyimide aerogel; secondly, CQDs could act as the electron transport medium, which facilitated the charge transfer among the polymer chains; thirdly, CQDs could effectively enhance π - π coupling between the conjuncted polyimide chains, which could in turn pave a way for the charge transfer [65]; fourthly, the heterojunction between polyimide aerogel and ZnS quantum dots could also favor the charge transfer. Thus the lifetime of the electron was prolonged, which facilitated the sepaeration of electron and hole and prohibited their recombination. It is noted that the fluorescence intensity of PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 aerogel was the lowest, which is expected that PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 aerogel possessed the best photocatalytic activity. When the content of ZnS was 3%, the fluorescence intensity of PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 was lower than that of PI/CQDs/ZnS-1. This is because the large aggregated particles could favor the recombination of photogenerated electron and hole.

Fig. 9(a) showed photocatalytic degradation curves of OTC over PI, PI/CQDs/ZnS-0.5, PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 and PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 aerogels. It could be seen that PI/CQDs/ZnS aerogel displayed increased photocatalytic performance compared to pure PI aerogel. After 180 min, the left concentration of the OTC solution catalyzed by the PI aerogel was 52%, and the photocatalytic rate was 48%. In contrast, the left concentration of OTC solution catalyzed by PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 was 27%, and the photocatalytic rate was 73%, confirming that the photocatalytic property of the polyimide aerogel was increased significantly. The improved photocatalytic activity of PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 was ascribed to large area surface, the enhanced visible absorption of the aerogel and the efficient separation of the photogenerated electron and hole. It could be seen that the photocatalytic property of PI/CQDs/ZnS-3 decreased compared to PI/CQDs/ZnS-1. The possible reason was that “shielding effect” of the excessive CQDs could decrease the light absorption and cover the active sites of the aerogels.

Besides of photocatalytic activity, the recycling stability is another important property to the photocatalyst. Thus the recycling stability of PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 was tested by repeating three cycles of OTC degradation. Fig. 9(b) showed the degradation of OTC over PI/CQDs-1 under visible light for three cycles. It could be seen that there was little change on the degradation rate after three cycles. The results confirmed that the PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 had recycling stability. The possible reason was that the polyimide aerogel had high structural stability and mechanical strength. The excellent property of the aerogel could keep its original morphology, which could prevent the loss and the aggregation of active catalysts during the recycling photocatalysis process.

The visible-light photocatalytic degradation curves over OTC of PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 aerogel with and without scavengers were obtained to study the photocatalytic mechanism. Fig. 10 showed the visible-light photocatalytic degradation curves over OTC of PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 aerogel with and without scavengers. It is reported by the article that active species, h+, ·O2 − and ·OH, could play the role in the oxidation of OTC. EDTA, benzoquinone and methanol were the corresponding scavengers. So the scavengers could be added to the OTC solution to study the photocatalytic mechanism. According to the photocatalytic degradation curves, the concentration of OTC was in order: EDTA > benzoquinone > methanol, showing that the role of the active species was in order: h+ > ·O2 − > ·OH.

Based on the result above, the photocatalytic degradation mechanism was proposed as shown in Fig. 11 . Owing to the large surface area and the porous 3D network, the PI/CQDs/ZnS aerogel could absorb the small molecule, such as the pollutant and O2, to the surface. Meanwhile, more active sites were exposed on the surface of the aerogels. Owing to the upconversion of CQDs, visible light was transferred to ultroviolet light, which enhanced the light absorption of the PI aerogel. The electron of the polyimide could absorb the light energy and be motived to the surface of the the aerogels more easily. The electron could reduce O2 to ·O2 −, which could play the role in the degradation. Owing to the electron escaping, h+ generated, which could oxidize the pollutant itself. The good conductivity of CQDs together with the heterojunction between PI aerogel and ZnS quantum dots could facilitate the separation of the photoexcited electron and hole, and thus prolong the lifetime of the electron, which increased the photocatalytic performances.

In summary，the polyimide/CQDs/ZnS quantum dots material aerogels were prepared by supercritical fluid drying technology for the first time. The surface area of PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 was 434 m2/g, increased by 185 m2/g compared to pure PI aerogel. The quantum dots increased the visible light absorption of the polyimide aerogel, speeded the charge transfer rate and facilitated the separation of the photogenerated electron and holes. PI/CQDs/ZnS-1 possessed excellent photocatalytic rate, which was 1.5 times greater than that of pure aerogel. This study could supply theoretical guidance for preparing the novel aerogel catalysts used to the photocatalytic degradation over the pollutant.
The data used to support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon request.

Qian Liu: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Zijie Fan: Methodology, Investigation, Visualization. Xibin Yi: Software, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Shiwei Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Bing Li: Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful for the supports of the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (Grant No. ZR2019BEM025); Young Doctor Cooperation Foundation of Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences) (Grant No. 2019BSHZ0016); College Students Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (Grant No. S201910431075; xj201810431071); Science-Education-Industry Integration Innovation Pilot Project of Qilu University of Technology (Grant No. 2020KJC-GH13).

Sci. Pollut. Res., 28 (2021) 49125–49138.
